data.title: Tariffs are a 'stagflationary shock,' Chicago Fed's Goolsbee said
data.description:
A tariff is a "stagflationary shock," which means it hurts both sides of the Federal Reserve's dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said Thursday.

"There's no playbook" for the Fed to respond to a stagflationary shock, he added.

Still, he believes that interest rates will decline over the next one to two years, but the economy will have to get through some bumps between now and then, he added.

"The Fed timetable is not the market timetable, and the stock market is not the economy, " Goolsbee said in opening remarks at a moderated conversation on Thursday.

"Our goal is to find the through-line," not to jump to conclusions, he said during an event at the Economic Club of New York. It appears that the stock market "has jumped to conclusions" ahead of everyone else. 

Meanwhile, the hard data still "looks pretty good," he said.

He discussed the reverberations of tariffs that can build on the effects of the tariffs on prices, such as how much of the tariffs producers pass through to users and the retaliatory tariffs imposed by other countries. 

Developing... check back for updates.

data.title: Summit Therapeutics spikes after co-CEO’s early warrants exercise
data.description:
Shares of Summit Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SMMT) traded higher for the second straight session on Thursday after Cantor Fitzgerald said that an earlier-than-expected warrant exercise by the company’s co-CEO, Bob Duggan, indicated a bullish signal for the stock.

According to a regulatory filing on Wednesday, Duggan, a venture capitalist who already owns 556M shares of the company (70% of ownership), exercised warrants to buy nearly 4.0M SMMT shares about four years before their expiration.

Another regulatory filing indicated that Summit’s (NASDAQ:SMMT) co-CEO and board president, Maky Zanganeh, procured a similar number of SMMT shares after exercising her warrants roughly four years in advance.

“In our view, there is only one reason to exercise these warrants early. Bob thinks SMMT shares will go higher,” Cantor analyst Eric Schmidt wrote with an Overweight rating on the stock.

Both transactions took place on Tuesday as the company prepares to release overall survival (OS) data from its partner Akeso’s (OTCPK:AKESF) HARMONi-2 Phase 3 trial for its lead asset, ivonescimab.

The PD-1/VEGF bispecific antibody has already outperformed Merck’s (MRK) blockbuster cancer therapy Keytruda (pembrolizumab) against lung cancer, according to data on progression-free survival (PFS) released from the China-based study last year.

“Much is riding on whether ivonescimab is superior to pembro in OS, given we have already seen very strong PFS data from this study,” Schmidt added.

data.title: Long-term mortgage rates remain below 7% for twelfth straight week
data.description:
Long-term mortgage rates continued their downward trajectory, remaining under 7% for the twelfth consecutive week, the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey showed.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.62% as of April 10, down from 6.64% last week and 6.88% in the same period a year ago.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.82%, unchanged from last week and lower than 6.16% a year ago.

"As purchase applications continue to climb, the spring homebuying season is shaping up to look more favorable than last year," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.

data.title: Amazon's Andy Jassy on TikTok U.S. bid: 'I don’t think we said that'
data.description:
Amazon's (NASDAQ:AMZN) top boss, Andy Jassy, said in an interview on CNBC Wednesday that the company did not say that it is not bidding for TikTok U.S.

The CEO of the e-commerce and tech giant did not comment on M&A rumors when asked further during the CNBC interview.

The statement by Jassy comes after several media reports from earlier this month that claimed that the company submitted a last-minute bid for the short video content app.

But when asked if TikTok is an important piece in the ongoing tariff conversation, Jassy said, "I don’t know... I think the conversations that the administration is having, probably was having before and more intensely is having now with each country are complicated. And I imagine that each have their set of variables and I’ve read that this may be one of the pieces in that negotiation, but I am not privy to it."

Jassy's statement comes after U.S. President Donald Trump further extended the deadline for the app by 75 days to keep it operational against the sale-or-ban law taking effect, which was passed under the Biden administration.

A Beijing spokesperson said Wednesday that any deal for TikTok U.S. has to be in compliance with Chinese law. China also indicated it would not approve any deal under Trump's reciprocal tariff threat.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades: TSLA, AAPL, NVDA, META, PLTR, ABBV, HUM, TGT
data.description:
A roundup of the most recent upgrades issued by Seeking Alpha analysts.

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA). Sell to hold by James Foord. "I recommend waiting for the next quarter's results and potential tariff resolutions before increasing TSLA stock holdings, targeting technical support levels around $160 or $100."

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA).  Sell to hold by Gary Alexander. "Tesla could prove an opportunistic buy now if the new Model Q vehicles, upon launch in mid-2025 (at least per the company's current official statement, though many are now expecting a delay) help to shift the conversation away from Musk's political activities and onto the cheaper product itself."

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA). Hold to buy by Bay Area Ideas. "I upgrade Nvidia stock to a buy rating as the technical weakness and undervaluation may be a buying opportunity even though near-term declines may persist."

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA). Sell to buy by MMMT Wealth.

Nvidia (NVDA). Hold to buy by Livy Investment Research.

Apple. (NASDAQ:AAPL). Sell to hold by Jonathan Weber. "Despite a recent valuation decline, AAPL is still not a bargain, trading at an earnings multiple of around 27, which is not especially attractive. Tariffs and trade tensions, particularly with China, pose substantial risks to the Company's supply chain and market demand, potentially impacting profitability."

Meta. (NASDAQ:META). Hold to buy by Dair Sansyzbayev.

AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV). Hold to buy by Stephen Ayers. "DCF analysis shows AbbVie is approximately 10% undervalued with a favorable risk/reward profile and an attractive 3.73% dividend yield."

Humana (NYSE:HUM). Sell to hold by Harrison Schwartz. "Humana is undervalued in the short term, but its long-term profitability is uncertain due to potential future cuts in Medicare Advantage spending and due to the rising fiscal deficit."

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR). Sell to hold by Deep Value Investing.

Target (NYSE:TGT). Hold to buy by Ken Taylor. "Target stock is undervalued, presenting a strong buy opportunity with the potential for 12.8% to 24.6% annualized total returns if management hits its targets."

Target (NYSE:TGT). Hold to buy by Amrita Roy. "I am upgrading Target stock to a "buy" with a price target of $115, representing a 31% upside, due to attractive risk-reward dynamics that have been created from the latest sell-off."

Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN). Hold to buy by Mike Zaccardi.

Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN). Hold to buy by Julian Lin.

Coinbase (COIN). Hold to buy by Michael Fay.

Albermarle (NYSE:ALB). Hold to buy by Juxtaposed Ideas.

MongoDB (NASDAQ:MDB). Hold to buy by Gary Alexander.

Elastic (NYSE:ESTC). Hold to buy by Eleceed Capital.

Constellation Energy (NASDAQ:CEG). Hold to buy by Laura Starks.

data.title: Wall Street back in selloff mode, Nasdaq slides 5% as tariff concerns still weigh
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages slumped on Thursday as tariff worries lingered, while the CPI came in cooler than expected in March.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was last -4.7% in midday trade, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -5.4%, and the Dow (DJI) was -3.8%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dipped 8 basis points to 4.32%. At the same time, the 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 13 basis points to 3.82%.

The Consumer Price Index came in at -0.1% M/M vs. +0.1% consensus, +2.4% Y/Y vs. +2.6% consensus, and +2.8% prior.

Core CPI came in at +0.1% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, +2.8% Y/Y vs. +3.0% consensus and +3.1% prior

It was nothing short of a historic day for markets on Wednesday, as U.S. President Donald Trump's pause on reciprocal tariffs against most countries except China sent Wall Street to its best day since the financial crisis of 2008. The S&P 500 rocketed +9.52%; the Nasdaq Composite exploded +12.16%, and the Dow surged +7.87% on Wednesday. 

"Market turmoil turned into elation yesterday as Trump announced a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for countries other than China, which marked a sizable backing off from last week’s tariff escalation," Deutsche Bank's Peter Sidorov said.

The European Union paused countermeasures on U.S. tariffs.

Trump on Wednesday said he would issue a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries who had reached out to the U.S. for trade negotiations.

Perhaps most crucially, we are currently still on course for a disorderly economic decoupling between the world’s two largest economies, with no immediate signs of either the U.S. or China backing down, Sidorov said.

Trump also upped the tariff rate against China to 125%. 

Recession risk in the US is lower than yesterday but remains high, UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Initial jobless claims edged up to 223K in the past week.

The Fed balance sheet will also land on Thursday.

data.title: Canada can become top LNG supplier to Asia, TC Energy CEO says
data.description:
TC Energy (NYSE:TRP) CEO Francois Poirier said Thursday he believes Canada can be the top exporter of liquefied natural gas to Asia, but political leadership is crucial to making it happen.

"We have the supply, we have a transportation cost advantage and the demand is there for the taking," Poirier said in a speech to Canadian Club Toronto.

.Gas produced in Western Canada could sell for a much higher price in Asia than if it were to remain landlocked, and securing new buyers would reduce Canada’s reliance on the U.S., proponents have said.

The initial phase of Canada’s first LNG export terminal in Kitimat, British Columbia, is expected to start up in the middle of this year; TC Energy (NYSE:TRP) built the Coastal GasLink pipeline that ships natural gas across B.C. to Kitimat.

"Canada was at the starting blocks with the U.S. in pursuing LNG exports" 15 years ago, proposing as many as 18 LNG projects off its west coast, Poirier said: "We had the opportunity then to be No. 1, and now we're playing catch up. Canada today is commissioning its first LNG facility, while the U.S. [has] become the largest exporter in the world."

Poirier also called for "hands-on political management" to ensure projects are built on time and on budget, and that the party that wins the April 28 federal election should ask business leaders for a list of priority projects that government, industry and indigenous leaders can work together to build with a sense of urgency.

data.title: Tariffs loom over Q1, but On Holdings and Deckers stand out - Raymond James
data.description:
As Q1 earnings from some of retail’s biggest names approach, Raymond James expects tariffs will dominate earnings calls and stock narratives but thinks it’s unlikely that the financial impacts will be provided given the rapidly changing environment.

As such, Raymond James has not changed its FY26 estimates but expects several names will be “hurt the most” given their exposure to tariffed countries and price elasticity. These include Beyond (BYON), American Eagle Outfitters (AEO), FIGS (FIG), Under Armour (UA, UAA), and Capri (CPRI).

While there has been a lot of talk around tariffs, the foreign exchange environment has become more bullish for U.S. companies thanks to the recent weakness of the U.S. dollar, especially versus the euro, British pound, and Japanese yen. This should prove to be an incremental positive for most companies versus prior 2025 guidance.

Within Raymond James’ coverage, On Holdings (NYSE:ONON) remains a Strong Buy as recent checks show direct-to-consumer growth was consistent in the first quarter versus Q4. Other positives include demand outstripping supply at the wholesale level, new product launches, and a large runway for revenue and margin expansion. Working against the company is its high level of sourcing out of Vietnam.

Deckers Outdoor (NYSE:DECK) is also in a strong position into fiscal fourth quarter results, earning an upgrade to Strong buy from Outperform. While the deceleration in HOKA sales is known, there still remains a “significant” long-term runway. Additionally, the company maintains a strong balance sheet, margins, and free cash flow.

“Our sum-of-the-parts supports a significantly higher stock,” Raymond James said in Thursday’s upgrade.  

Despite the vote of confidence for On Holdings (NYSE:ONON) and Deckers Outdoor (NYSE:DECK), both are trading more than 6% lower on Thursday in sympathy with category weakness as profit-taking off Wednesday’s meteoric rally leaves the footwear sector broadly lower.

data.title: Broadcom, Nvidia lead chip tumble as market sell-off resumes
data.description:
One day after a historic rally on Wall Street, semiconductor stocks resumed a deep slide on Thursday, led by sharp drops in Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) and Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA).

Broadcom fell 7% shortly before midday, while Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) fell more than 5%. Only 24 hours prior, shares of both companies rose more than 10% as President Trump agreed to pause most tariffs for 90 days while his administration works on trade deals with most countries.

Separately on Wednesday, it was reported that the White House had stepped back from banning all of Nvidia's H20 GPUs to China, which had previously been under consideration.

Other semiconductor stocks also saw sharp drops in selling on Thursday, including AMD (AMD), Intel (INTC), Marvell (MRVL) and Texas Instruments (TXN), which fell 8%, 7%, 12% and 8.5%, respectively. Qualcomm (QCOM), Micron Technology (MU), and Analog Devices (ADI) all fell 6% or more shortly before noon.

The widely watched Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) sank 8.5%, broadly in line with a number of its components.

Some analysts have expressed concern that the tariffs implemented by President Trump have upended the global tech supply chain and could lead to many companies withholding guidance during the upcoming earnings season.

data.title: Patterson Cos. ticks lower amid report debt financing for sale delayed
data.description:
Patterson Cos. (NASDAQ:PDCO) edged lower by 0.9% amid a report that debt financing for its planned sale to Patient Square has been delayed amid recent market volatility.

A $2.35 billion debt package for the deal has been delayed, according to a Bloomberg report on Thursday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Patterson is evaluating amending the terms on bond-and-loan financing for its planned $3.4 billion sale to Patient Square. 

Banks on the transaction are considering sweetening terms to help account for volatility in the market due to the current tariff spat, according to the report. A deadline to commit to a $1.35 billion leverage loan was on Tuesday, while a $1 billion junk-bond sale was expected to be priced on Wednesday, though has not. 

Patterson Cos. (NASDAQ:PDCO) has said the sale to Patient Square is set to close later this month. 

Patient Square declined to comment to Bloomberg, while Patterson didn't respond to requests for comment from Bloomberg and Seeking Alpha.
